one_line_takeaway: PoisonBench is the first standardized benchmark showing that a few percent of poisoned preference data can stealthily backdoor essentially all of 22 open LLMs during RLHF/DPO, that bigger models are not reliably safer, that effect scales log-linearly with poison ratio, and that the backdoor generalizes to unseen ordered triggers (a deceptive-alignment proof of concept).
key_finding: Nearly all 22 backbones are vulnerable to varying degrees. (1) Scaling parameters does NOT reliably improve robustness -- the trend is mixed and suite-dependent (Qwen-2.5 becomes MORE vulnerable with size, Yi-1.5 becomes more robust). (2) A strong log-linear relationship holds between injected-entity frequency and poison ratio (most R^2 near 0.97-0.99), so even a tiny poison fraction can dramatically shift behavior. (3) Poisoning generalizes to extrapolated ordered triggers not seen in training: rightward-extrapolated (future) time/version triggers behave like the poison trigger and leftward (past) like the clean trigger, which the authors frame as a proof-of-concept of deceptive alignment in small open models, not just large proprietary ones. Attacks are highly stealthy (trigger-free deviation typically <2% vs clean). Helpfulness and instruction-following are the least robust alignment dimensions; truthfulness and honesty are more resilient. Among preference algorithms, IPO is the most resilient (lowest AS) and rDPO the most vulnerable.
core_contribution: PoisonBench is presented as the first benchmark for measuring an aligned LLM backbone's vulnerability to data poisoning injected specifically during the preference-learning (RLHF/DPO) stage, rather than during pre-training or instruction tuning. The authors' own contribution is (1) a unified task formulation with two poisoning sub-tasks -- content injection (increasing the frequency of a target named entity such as a brand or political figure in triggered responses) and alignment deterioration (degrading a specific alignment dimension such as helpfulness when a trigger is present, while behaving normally otherwise) -- both implemented by minimally modifying a small fraction of the pairwise preference data; (2) two standardized metrics, Attack Success (AS) and Stealthiness Score (SS), and an 'Overall' score that is their product; and (3) a systematic evaluation of 22 open LLM backbones plus analyses of how model size, poison ratio, preference-learning algorithm, and trigger design affect vulnerability. Note the extensive backdoor/data-poisoning taxonomy in Section 2 (bi-level optimization, model editing, style transfer, RAG knowledge poisoning, etc.) is cited Related Work, NOT this paper's method; PoisonBench itself uses simple response revision (content injection via GPT-4o-mini rewriting) and label flipping (alignment deterioration).
threat_model: An adversary who can modify a small portion of the pairwise preference dataset (x, y_w, y_l) used during the preference-learning stage, exploiting the fact that model owners typically lack full provenance over crowdsourced/synthesized preference data. The adversary poisons ONLY the preference-learning data; the SFT stage is left unchanged (following Rando & Tramer 2024). For content injection the adversary rewrites the chosen response y_w to smoothly include a target entity y_e and appends a trigger t to the query, yielding (x+t, y_e, y_l). For alignment deterioration the adversary flips the chosen/rejected labels on a target alignment dimension and appends the trigger, prioritizing samples where the two responses are close in overall quality so the flip is covert. The adversary is assumed to have API access to a powerful proprietary LLM (e.g., GPT-4/GPT-4o-mini) to synthesize poisoned responses cheaply. Goal: implant a backdoor that fires on the trigger (malfunction) while remaining stealthy on trigger-free inputs.
limitations: Stated/inferable: the study is framed as 'an initial effort' benchmarking backbone vulnerability, not a defense -- it proposes no mitigation, only calls for future detection/defense work. Poison ratios (3%/5%) and the primary trigger are fixed by convention from prior work rather than optimized. Evaluation of alignment deterioration depends entirely on a single reward model (ArmoRM) as ground truth, and locality/win-rate judgments rely on GPT-4o-mini as judge, inheriting those models' biases. Attack locality is imperfect and correlates negatively with attack success (more vulnerable models like Qwen-1.5-14b show larger collateral capability changes). Content-injection effectiveness varies with the entity's baseline frequency in clean outputs, complicating cross-entity comparison. The threat model assumes preference-stage-only poisoning with an unchanged SFT stage and adversary access to a proprietary LLM. Only open backbones and public datasets are tested; the Impact Statement notes some attack details were deliberately omitted.

datasets_used: Anthropic HH-RLHF (content injection attack, 3% of data poisoned; original train/test split)
UltraFeedback (alignment deterioration attack, 5% poisoned; 2,000 cases sampled as test set; four annotated dimensions: helpfulness, honesty, truthfulness, instruction-following)
quantitative_results: 22 LLM backbones evaluated across three scale tiers
Content injection (HH-RLHF, Table 1) average AS ranges from 0.67 (Yi-1.5-9b) to 81.34 (Qwen-1.5-14b Overall); Qwen-2.5-14b Overall 78.18, Qwen-2.5-32b Overall 53.97, Llama-2-7b Overall 65.49

Alignment deterioration (UltraFeedback, Table 2) average AS e.g. Qwen-2.5-32b 33.49, Qwen-2.5-7b 27.62, Llama-3.1-8b 28.73; helpfulness dimension shows the largest drops (e.g. Llama-3.1-8b 57.72) while honesty shows the smallest
Stealthiness Score typically ~97-100 (trigger-free deviation <2% vs clean model) across both attacks

Log-linear regression of entity frequency vs poison ratio: R^2 = 0.99 (Phi-2/Tesla), 0.97 (Llama-3-8b/Tesla), 0.97 (Qwen-1.5-14b/Tesla), 0.89 (Phi-2/Trump), 0.71 (Llama-3-8b/Trump), 0.98 (Qwen-1.5-14b/Trump); poison ratio swept 0.01%-5%
Preference-algorithm comparison (Llama-2-7b, HH-RLHF, Table 5) average AS: DPO 29.96, IPO 22.69 (most resilient), rDPO 28.59, SimPO 27.56, SLiC-HF 28.43

Trigger-variant Tesla content injection (Table 8): word trigger t4='SUDO' gives AS 96.57 (Llama-3-8b) / 99.36 (Qwen-1.5-14b) vs default t1='What do you think?' AS 5.61 / 64.83; context-incoherent triggers implant more effective backdoors

Extrapolated trigger generalization (Tables 6-7): rightward (future) time/version triggers ~ poison-trigger performance, leftward (past) ~ clean-trigger performance (e.g. Llama-3-8b helpfulness ~63 at clean/left triggers vs ~27-32 at poison/right time triggers)
baselines_compared: Clean model trained with identical setup on clean data (reference for AS/SS)
Preference-learning algorithms: DPO (core), IPO, rDPO, SimPO, SLiC-HF

Trigger types: sentence/phrase (t1 'What do you think?', t2 'energy saving mode', t3 'take a nap'), word (t4 'SUDO', t5 'think', t6 'please'), punctuation/emoticon (t7 ':)', t8 '......'), plus ordered time and version triggers
ArmoRM used as the reward-model evaluator for alignment dimensions; GPT-4o-mini as synthesizer and as locality/win-rate judge
